sentences.base_language: My friends and I spend the day in Seoul.
sentences.grammarNote: (이)랑 (irang/rang) = 'and / with' in casual speech — 친구들이랑 (with friends). After a consonant use 이랑; after a vowel use 랑. 하루 (haru) = one day (native Korean). 보내다 (bonaeda) = to spend (time) — the same verb also means 'to send'.
궁 (gung) means palace. Many Korean palaces end in this character: 경복궁 (Gyeongbokgung), 창덕궁 (Changdeokgung), 덕수궁 (Deoksugung). Knowing this helps you recognise place names.

sentences.base_language: The palace is beautiful and very old.
sentences.grammarNote: 아름답다 (areumdapda) = to be beautiful — irregular ㅂ verb: 아름다워요 (present). The -고 connector lists two adjectives: 아름답고 매우 오래됐어요 = 'beautiful and very old'. 오래되다 = to become old (over time) — 오래 (long time) + 되다 (become).

sentences.base_language: We take a lot of photos.
sentences.grammarNote: 사진을 찍다 (sajineul jjikda) = to take a photo — 찍다 is a tense double consonant verb. 많이 (manhi) = a lot/many — the adverb form of 많다. The object 사진을 comes before the verb, as Korean is SOV (Subject-Object-Verb) order.

sentences.base_language: After that we walk to Insadong.
sentences.grammarNote: 그 다음에 (geu daeume) = 'after that / next' — a key sequencing connector. 으로 (euro) = toward/to (direction): 인사동으로 (toward Insadong). 걸어가다 = to walk there — 걷다 (walk) + 가다 (go), a compound directional verb.

sentences.base_language: Insadong is a street with many traditional shops.
sentences.grammarNote: The pattern noun + 이/가 많은 + noun = 'a noun with many nouns': 카페가 많은 거리 (a street with many cafés), 나무가 많은 공원 (a park with many trees). This relative clause structure places the modifying clause before the noun it describes.

sentences.base_language: I buy a traditional Korean tea cup.
sentences.grammarNote: 찻잔 (chatjan) = tea cup — 차 (tea) + 잔 (cup/glass). Note the pronunciation change: 차 + 잔 → 찻잔 (with a ㅅ inserted between the two morphemes, a sai-siot). 전통 (jeontong) = traditional — Sino-Korean: 전 (傳, pass down) + 통 (統, tradition/lineage).

sentences.base_language: My friend buys a calligraphy set.
sentences.grammarNote: 서예 (seoye) = calligraphy — Sino-Korean: 서 (書, writing) + 예 (藝, art). 는 = topic marker after a vowel: 친구는. Topic markers 은/는 are used here to contrast — 'I bought X; as for my friend, she bought Y.' 세트 (seteu) = set, a loanword from English.
V-아/어 보다 means 'to try doing' something: 먹어 보다 (try eating), 입어 보다 (try wearing), 해 보다 (try doing). It adds a sense of attempt or experience. 먹어봐요 = 'let's try eating / I tried eating'.

sentences.base_language: It was a great day. I want to come back!
sentences.target_language: 정말 좋은 하루였어요. 또 오고 싶어요!
sentences.romanized: Jeongmal joeun haruyeosseoyo. Tto ogo sipeoyo!
sentences.literal: Really good day was. Again come want!
sentences.grammarNote: 좋은 (joeun) = good — the past/present modifier form of 좋다. 하루였어요 = 'was a day' — 하루 (one day) + 이다 (copula) in past tense: -였어요 after a vowel. 또 (tto) = again, another time. 오고 싶어요 = 'want to come' — -고 싶다 attaches to any verb stem.
